items.title: Start with a baseline before going deep
items.avoid: Jumping straight to a large pretrained transformer for every problem.
items.prefer: Train a simple ML or small neural baseline on the same split to quantify lift from depth and representation learning.
items.why: Baselines reveal whether data size, noise, or labels — not model class — limit performance.
items.example: Compare logistic regression vs a two-hidden-layer MLP on the same CSV split before importing a vision foundation model.

items.title: Match architecture family to data modality
items.avoid: Feeding raw images into a plain fully connected stack without spatial structure.
items.prefer: Use convolutional or attention architectures suited to images, sequences, or graphs so inductive bias matches the data.
items.why: Representation learning still needs structural priors; depth alone does not fix wrong architecture choices.
items.example: MNIST with a small CNN typically learns faster than flattening pixels into a huge MLP.

items.title: Plan for data volume and quality
items.avoid: Assuming unlabeled or mislabeled data will be fixed automatically by depth.
items.prefer: Audit labels, balance classes, augment carefully, and monitor validation metrics before scaling parameters.
items.why: DL amplifies patterns in data — including spurious correlations and annotation errors.
items.example: Medical classifier trained on images with hospital-specific watermarks learns site ID instead of pathology.
